title: Integration with partial fractions
description: Integration of rational functions by division and partial fractions practice problems

hints:
The [[☃ definition 1]] is a rational function where the degree of the numerator is smaller than the degree of the denominator. Also, the denominator is factorable. This suggests that we should rewrite the integrand using partial fractions:

$\displaystyle\int\dfrac{x+5}{(x+2)(x-1)}\, dx=\int\left(\dfrac{A}{x+2}+\dfrac{B}{x-1}\right)\,dx$

Then, we will be able to integrate using the following formula, which is based on the derivative of $\text{ln}(x)$:

$\displaystyle\int\dfrac{1}{mx+n}\,dx=\dfrac1m\cdot\text{ln}|mx+n|+C$

We can now rewrite the integrand and integrate the expression:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int\dfrac{x+5}{(x+2)(x-1)}\,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int\left(\dfrac{\blueD A}{x+2}+\dfrac{\maroonD B}{x-1}\right)\,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int\left(\dfrac{\blueD{-1}}{x+2}+\dfrac{\maroonD{2}}{x-1}\,\right)\,dx
\\\\
&=-1\displaystyle\int\dfrac{1}{x+2}\, dx+2\int\dfrac{1}{x-1}\, dx
\\\\
&=-1\text{ln}|x+2|+2\text{ln}|x-1|+C
\\\\
&=2\text{ln}|x-1|-\text{ln}|x+2|+C
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle\int\dfrac{x+5}{(x+2)(x-1)}\, dx = 2\text{ln}|x-1|-\text{ln}|x+2|+C$

The [[☃ definition 1]] is a rational function where the degree of the numerator is smaller than the degree of the denominator. Also, the denominator is factorable. This suggests that we should rewrite the integrand using partial fractions:

$\displaystyle\int\dfrac{3x+1}{x(x+1)}\,dx=\int\left(\dfrac{A}{x}+\dfrac{B}{x+1}\right)\,dx$

Then, we will be able to integrate using the following formula, which is based on the derivative of $\text{ln}(x)$:

$\displaystyle\int\dfrac{1}{mx+n}\,dx=\dfrac1m\cdot\text{ln}|mx+n|+C$

We can now rewrite the integrand and integrate the expression:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int\dfrac{3x+1}{x(x+1)}\,dx
\\\\
&= \displaystyle\int\left(\dfrac{\blueD A}{x}+\dfrac{\maroonD B}{x+1}\right)\, dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int\left(\dfrac{\blueD{1}}{x}+\dfrac{\maroonD{2}}{x+1}\, \right)dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int\dfrac{1}{x}\, dx+2\int\dfrac{1}{x+1}\, dx
\\\\
&=\text{ln}|x|+2\text{ln}|x+1|+C
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle\int\dfrac{3x+1}{x(x+1)}\, dx=\text{ln}|x|+2\text{ln}|x+1|+C$

The [[☃ definition 1]] is a rational function where the degree of the numerator is smaller than the degree of the denominator. Also, the denominator is factorable. This suggests that we should rewrite the integrand using partial fractions:

$\displaystyle\int\dfrac{2x+3}{(x-3)(x+3)}\,dx=\int\left(\dfrac{A}{x-3}+\dfrac{B}{x+3}\right)\,dx$

Then, we will be able to integrate using the following formula, which is based on the derivative of $\text{ln}(x)$:

$\displaystyle\int\dfrac{1}{mx+n}\,dx=\dfrac1m\cdot\text{ln}|mx+n|+C$

We can now rewrite the integrand and integrate the expression:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int\dfrac{2x+3}{(x-3)(x+3)}\,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int\left(\dfrac{\blueD A}{x-3}+\dfrac{\maroonD B}{x+3}\right)\, dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int\left(\dfrac{\blueD{\dfrac32}}{x-3}+\dfrac{\maroonD{\dfrac12}}{x+3}\, \right)dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\dfrac32\int\dfrac{1}{x-3}\, dx+\dfrac12\int\dfrac{1}{x+3}\, dx
\\\\
&=\dfrac32\text{ln}|x-3|+\dfrac12\text{ln}|x+3|+C
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle \int\dfrac{2x+3}{(x-3)(x+3)} \,dx = \dfrac32\text{ln}|x-3|+\dfrac12\text{ln}|x+3|+C$

The [[☃ definition 1]] is a rational function where the degree of the numerator is smaller than the degree of the denominator. Also, the denominator is factorable. This suggests that we should rewrite the integrand using partial fractions:

$\displaystyle\int\dfrac{1}{(x+2)(x-2)}\,dx=\int\left(\dfrac{A}{x+2}+\dfrac{B}{x-2}\right)\,dx$

Then, we will be able to integrate using the following formula, which is based on the derivative of $\text{ln}(x)$:

$\displaystyle\int\dfrac{1}{mx+n}\,dx=\dfrac1m\cdot\text{ln}|mx+n|+C$

We can now rewrite the integrand and integrate the expression:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int\dfrac{1}{(x+2)(x-2)}\,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int\left(\dfrac{\blueD A}{x+2}+\dfrac{\maroonD B}{x-2}\right)\, dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int\left(\dfrac{\blueD{-\dfrac14}}{x+2}+\dfrac{\maroonD{\dfrac14}}{x-2}\, \right)dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle-\dfrac14\int\dfrac{1}{x+2}\, dx+\dfrac14\int\dfrac{1}{x-2}\, dx
\\\\
&=-\dfrac14\text{ln}|x+2|+\dfrac14\text{ln}|x-2|+C
\\\\
&=\dfrac14\text{ln}|x-2|-\dfrac14\text{ln}|x+2|+C
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle \int\dfrac{1}{(x+2)(x-2)} \,dx =\dfrac14\text{ln}|x-2|-\dfrac14\text{ln}|x+2|+C$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find the values of $P$ and $Q$ that make the following equality true.**

$\displaystyle \int\dfrac{1-2x}{(x+1)(x-5)} \,dx=P\cdot\text{ln}|x+1|+Q\cdot\text{ln}|x-5|+C$

$P=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]

$Q=$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]]
hints:
The [[☃ definition 1]] is a rational function where the degree of the numerator is smaller than the degree of the denominator. Also, the denominator is factorable. This suggests that we should rewrite the integrand using partial fractions:

$\displaystyle\int\dfrac{1-2x}{(x+1)(x-5)}\,dx=\int\left(\dfrac{A}{x+1}+\dfrac{B}{x-5}\right)\,dx$

Then, we will be able to integrate using the following formula, which is based on the derivative of $\text{ln}(x)$:

$\displaystyle\int\dfrac{1}{mx+n}\,dx=\dfrac1m\cdot\text{ln}|mx+n|+C$

To find $A$ and $B$, we take the following equation and multiply both sides by the common denominator:

$\begin{align}
\displaystyle\dfrac{1-2x}{(x+1)(x-5)}&=\dfrac{A}{x+1}+\dfrac{B}{x-5}
\\\\
\displaystyle 1-2x&=A(x-5)+B(x+1)
\end{align}$

Substituting $x=-1$, we get $\blueD{A=-\dfrac12}$.

Substituting $x=5$, we get $\maroonD{B=-\dfrac32}$.

We can now rewrite the integrand and integrate the expression:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int\dfrac{1-2x}{(x+1)(x-5)}\,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int\left(\dfrac{\blueD A}{x+1}+\dfrac{\maroonD B}{x-5}\right)\, dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int\left(\dfrac{\blueD{-\dfrac12}}{x+1}+\dfrac{\maroonD{-\dfrac32}}{x-5}\, \right)dx
\\\\
&=-\dfrac12\displaystyle\int\dfrac{1}{x+1}\, dx-\dfrac32\int\dfrac{1}{x-5}\, dx
\\\\
&=-\dfrac12\text{ln}|x+1|-\dfrac32\text{ln}|x-5|+C
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle \int\dfrac{1-2x}{(x+1)(x-5)}\,dx=-\dfrac12\text{ln}|x+1|-\dfrac32\text{ln}|x-5|+C$

So $P=-\dfrac12$ and $Q=-\dfrac32$.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find the values of $P$ and $Q$ that make the following equality true.**

$\displaystyle \int\dfrac{x-3}{(x+4)(x+5)} \,dx = P\cdot\ln|x+4|+Q\cdot\ln|x+5|+C$

$P=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]

$Q=$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]]
hints:
The [[☃ definition 1]] is a rational function where the degree of the numerator is smaller than the degree of the denominator. Also, the denominator is factorable. This suggests that we should rewrite the integrand using partial fractions:

$\displaystyle\int\dfrac{x-3}{(x+4)(x+5)}\,dx=\int\left(\dfrac{A}{x+4}+\dfrac{B}{x+5}\right)\,dx$

Then, we will be able to integrate using the following formula, which is based on the derivative of $\text{ln}(x)$:

$\displaystyle\int\dfrac{1}{mx+n}\,dx=\dfrac1m\cdot\text{ln}|mx+n|+C$

We can now rewrite the integrand and integrate the expression:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int\dfrac{x-3}{(x+4)(x+5)}\,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int\left(\dfrac{\blueD A}{x+4}+\dfrac{\maroonD B}{x+5}\right)\, dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int\left(\dfrac{\blueD{-7}}{x+4}+\dfrac{\maroonD{8}}{x+5}\, \right)dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle-7\int\dfrac{1}{x+4}\, dx+8\int\dfrac{1}{x+5}\, dx
\\\\
&=-7\text{ln}|x+4|+8\text{ln}|x+5|+C
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle \int\dfrac{x-3}{(x+4)(x+5)} \,dx = -7\text{ln}|x+4|+8\text{ln}|x+5|+C$

So $P=-7$ and $Q=8$.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find the values of $P$ and $Q$ that make the following equality true.**

$\displaystyle \int\dfrac{2-4x}{(x+2)(x-3)} \,dx = P\cdot\ln|x+2|+Q\cdot\ln|x-3|+C$

$P=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]

$Q=$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]]
hints:
The [[☃ definition 1]] is a rational function where the degree of the numerator is smaller than the degree of the denominator. Also, the denominator is factorable. This suggests that we should rewrite the integrand using partial fractions:

$\displaystyle\int\dfrac{2-4x}{(x+2)(x-3)}\,dx=\int\left(\dfrac{A}{x+2}+\dfrac{B}{x-3}\right)\,dx$

Then, we will be able to integrate using the following formula, which is based on the derivative of $\text{ln}(x)$:

$\displaystyle\int\dfrac{1}{mx+n}\,dx=\dfrac1m\cdot\text{ln}|mx+n|+C$

To find $A$ and $B$, we take the following equation and multiply both sides by the common denominator:

$\begin{align}
\displaystyle\dfrac{2-4x}{(x+2)(x-3)}&=\dfrac{A}{x+2}+\dfrac{B}{x-3}
\\\\
2-4x&=A(x-3)+B(x+2)
\end{align}$

Substituting $x=-2$, we get $\blueD{A=-2}$.

Substituting $x=3$, we get $\maroonD{B=-2}$.

We can now rewrite the integrand and integrate the expression:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int\dfrac{2-4x}{(x+2)(x-3)}\,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int\left(\dfrac{\blueD A}{x+2}+\dfrac{\maroonD B}{x-3}\right)\, dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int\left(\dfrac{\blueD{-2}}{x+2}+\dfrac{\maroonD{-2}}{x-3}\, \right)dx
\\\\
&=-2\displaystyle\int\dfrac{1}{x+2}\, dx-2\int\dfrac{1}{x-3}\, dx
\\\\
&=-2\text{ln}|x+2|-2\text{ln}|x-3|+C
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle \int\dfrac{2-4x}{(x+2)(x-3)} \,dx =-2\text{ln}|x+2|-2\text{ln}|x-3|+C$

So $P=-2$ and $Q=-2$.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find the values of $P$ and $Q$ that make the following equality true.**

$\displaystyle \int\dfrac{x+8}{x(x+6)} \,dx = P\cdot\ln|x|+Q\cdot\ln|x+6|+C$

$P=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]

$Q=$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]]
hints:
The [[☃ definition 1]] is a rational function where the degree of the numerator is smaller than the degree of the denominator. Also, the denominator is factorable. This suggests that we should rewrite the integrand using partial fractions:

$\displaystyle\int\dfrac{x+8}{x(x+6)}\,dx=\int\left(\dfrac{A}{x}+\dfrac{B}{x+6}\right)\,dx$

Then, we will be able to integrate using the following formula, which is based on the derivative of $\text{ln}(x)$:

$\displaystyle\int\dfrac{1}{mx+n}\,dx=\dfrac1m\cdot\text{ln}|mx+n|+C$

To find $A$ and $B$, we take the following equation and multiply both sides by the common denominator:

$\begin{align}
\displaystyle\dfrac{x+8}{x(x+6)}&=\dfrac{A}{x}+\dfrac{B}{x+6}
\\\\
x+8&=A(x+6)+Bx
\end{align}$

Substituting $x=0$, we get $\blueD{A=\dfrac43}$.

Substituting $x=-6$, we get $\maroonD{B=-\dfrac13}$.

We can now rewrite the integrand and integrate the expression:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int\dfrac{x+8}{x(x+6)}\,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int\left(\dfrac{\blueD A}{x}+\dfrac{\maroonD B}{x+6}\right)\, dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int\left(\dfrac{\blueD{\dfrac43}}{x}+\dfrac{\maroonD{-\dfrac13}}{x+6}\, \right)dx
\\\\
&=\dfrac43\displaystyle\int\dfrac{1}{x}\, dx-\dfrac13\int\dfrac{1}{x+6}\, dx
\\\\
&=\dfrac43\text{ln}|x|-\dfrac13\text{ln}|x+6|+C
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle \int\dfrac{x+8}{x(x+6)} \,dx =\dfrac43\text{ln}|x|-\dfrac13\text{ln}|x+6|+C$

So $P=\dfrac43$ and $Q=-\dfrac13$.

The [[☃ definition 1]] is a rational function where the degree of the numerator is smaller than the degree of the denominator. Also, the denominator is factorable. This suggests that we should rewrite the integrand using partial fractions:

$\displaystyle\int\dfrac{x-2}{(2x+1)(x+3)}\,dx=\int\left(\dfrac{A}{2x+1}+\dfrac{B}{x+3}\right)\,dx$

Then, we will be able to integrate using the following formula, which is based on the derivative of $\text{ln}(x)$:

$\displaystyle\int\dfrac{1}{mx+n}\,dx=\dfrac1m\cdot\text{ln}|mx+n|+C$

We can now rewrite the integrand and integrate the expression:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int\dfrac{x-2}{(2x+1)(x+3)}\,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int\left(\dfrac{\blueD A}{2x+1}+\dfrac{\maroonD B}{x+3}\right)\,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int\dfrac{\blueD{-1}}{2x+1}+\dfrac{\maroonD{1}}{x+3}\,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle-1\int\dfrac{1}{2x+1}\,dx+\int\dfrac{1}{x+3}\, dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle-1\cdot\goldD{\dfrac12}\int\dfrac{\goldD{2}}{2x+1}\, dx+\int\dfrac{1}{x+3}\, dx
\\\\
&=-\dfrac12\text{ln}|2x+1|+\text{ln}|x+3|+C
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle \int\dfrac{x-2}{(2x+1)(x+3)} \,dx = -\dfrac12\text{ln}|2x+1|+\text{ln}|x+3|+C$

The [[☃ definition 1]] is a rational function where the degree of the numerator is smaller than the degree of the denominator. Also, the denominator is factorable. This suggests that we should rewrite the integrand using partial fractions:

$\displaystyle\int\dfrac{4x-11}{(2x-1)(x+4)}\, dx=\int\left(\dfrac{A}{2x-1}+\dfrac{B}{x+4}\right)\,dx$

Then, we will be able to integrate using the following formula, which is based on the derivative of $\text{ln}(x)$:

$\displaystyle\int\dfrac{1}{mx+n}\,dx=\dfrac1m\cdot\text{ln}|mx+n|+C$

We can now rewrite the integrand and integrate the expression:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int\dfrac{4x-11}{(2x-1)(x+4)} \,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int\left(\dfrac{\blueD A}{2x-1}+\dfrac{\maroonD B}{x+4}\right)\,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int\dfrac{\blueD{-2}}{2x-1}+\dfrac{\maroonD{3}}{x+4}\, dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle-2\cdot\goldD{\dfrac12}\int\dfrac{\goldD{2}}{2x-1}\, dx+3\int\dfrac{1}{x+4}\, dx
\\\\
&=-1\text{ln}|2x-1|+3\text{ln}|x+4|+C
\\\\
&=3\text{ln}|x+4|-\text{ln}|2x-1|+C
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle \int\dfrac{4x-11}{(2x-1)(x+4)} \,dx = 3\text{ln}|x+4|-\text{ln}|2x-1|+C$

The [[☃ definition 1]] is a rational function where the degree of the numerator is smaller than the degree of the denominator. Also, the denominator is factorable. This suggests that we should rewrite the integrand using partial fractions:

$\displaystyle\int\dfrac{1}{(x+1)(3x+1)}\, dx=\int\left(\dfrac{A}{x+1}+\dfrac{B}{3x+1}\right)\,dx$

Then, we will be able to integrate using the following formula, which is based on the derivative of $\text{ln}(x)$:

$\displaystyle\int\dfrac{1}{mx+n}\,dx=\dfrac1m\cdot\text{ln}|mx+n|+C$

To find $A$ and $B$, we take the following equation and multiply both sides by the common denominator:

$\begin{align}
\displaystyle\dfrac{1}{(x+1)(3x+1)}&=\dfrac{A}{x+1}+\dfrac{B}{3x+1}
\\\\
\displaystyle 1&=A(3x+1)+B(x+1)
\end{align}$

Substituting $x=-1$, we get $\blueD{A=-\dfrac12}$.

Substituting $x=-\dfrac13$, we get $\maroonD{B=\dfrac32}$.

We can now rewrite the integrand and integrate the expression:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int\dfrac{1}{(x+1)(3x+1)} \,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int\left(\dfrac{\blueD A}{x+1}+\dfrac{\maroonD B}{3x+1}\right)\,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int\dfrac{\blueD{-\dfrac12}}{x+1}+\dfrac{\maroonD{\dfrac32}}{3x+1}\, dx
\\\\
&=-\dfrac12\displaystyle\int\dfrac{1}{x+1}\, dx+\dfrac32\cdot\goldD{\dfrac13}\int\dfrac{\goldD{3}}{3x+1}\, dx
\\\\
&=-\dfrac12\text{ln}|x+1|+\dfrac12\text{ln}|3x+1|+C
\\\\
&=\dfrac12\text{ln}|3x+1|-\dfrac12\text{ln}|x+1|+C
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle \int\dfrac{1}{(x+1)(3x+1)} \,dx =\dfrac12\text{ln}|3x+1|-\dfrac12\text{ln}|x+1|+C$

The [[☃ definition 1]] is a rational function where the degree of the numerator is smaller than the degree of the denominator. Also, the denominator is factorable. This suggests that we should rewrite the integrand using partial fractions:

$\displaystyle\int\dfrac{14x-1}{(4x+1)(x-2)}\, dx=\int\left(\dfrac{A}{4x+1}+\dfrac{B}{x-2}\right)\,dx$

Then, we will be able to integrate using the following formula, which is based on the derivative of $\text{ln}(x)$:

$\displaystyle\int\dfrac{1}{mx+n}\,dx=\dfrac1m\cdot\text{ln}|mx+n|+C$

To find $A$ and $B$, we take the following equation and multiply both sides by the common denominator:

$\begin{align}
\displaystyle\dfrac{14x-1}{(4x+1)(x-2)}&=\dfrac{A}{4x+1}+\dfrac{B}{x-2}
\\\\
\displaystyle 14x-1&=A(x-2)+B(4x+1)
\end{align}$

Substituting $x=-\dfrac14$, we get $\blueD{A=2}$.

Substituting $x=2$, we get $\maroonD{B=3}$.

We can now rewrite the integrand and integrate the expression:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int\dfrac{14x-1}{(4x+1)(x-2)} \,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int\left(\dfrac{\blueD A}{4x+1}+\dfrac{\maroonD B}{x-2}\right)\,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int\dfrac{\blueD{2}}{4x+1}+\dfrac{\maroonD{3}}{x-2}\, dx
\\\\
&=2\cdot\goldD{\dfrac14}\displaystyle\int\dfrac{\goldD{4}}{4x+1}\, dx+3\int\dfrac{1}{x-2}\, dx
\\\\
&=\dfrac12\text{ln}|4x+1|+3\text{ln}|x-2|+C
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle \int\dfrac{14x-1}{(4x+1)(x-2)} \,dx =\dfrac12\text{ln}|4x+1|+3\text{ln}|x-2|+C$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find the values of $P$ and $Q$ that make the following equality true.**

$\displaystyle \int\dfrac{2}{x(3x-8)} \,dx = P\cdot\ln|x|+Q\cdot\ln|3x-8|+C$

$P=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]

$Q=$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]]
hints:
The [[☃ definition 1]] is a rational function where the degree of the numerator is smaller than the degree of the denominator. Also, the denominator is factorable. This suggests that we should rewrite the integrand using partial fractions:

$\displaystyle\int\dfrac{2}{x(3x-8)}\,dx=\int\left(\dfrac{A}{x}+\dfrac{B}{3x-8}\right)\,dx$

Then, we will be able to integrate using the following formula, which is based on the derivative of $\text{ln}(x)$:

$\displaystyle\int\dfrac{1}{mx+n}\,dx=\dfrac1m\cdot\text{ln}|mx+n|+C$

To find $A$ and $B$, we take the following equation and multiply both sides by the common denominator:

$\begin{align}
\displaystyle\dfrac{2}{x(3x-8)}&=\dfrac{A}{x}+\dfrac{B}{3x-8}
\\\\
2&=A(3x-8)+Bx
\end{align}$

Substituting $x=0$, we get $\blueD{A=-\dfrac14}$.

Substituting $x=\dfrac83$, we get $\maroonD{B=\dfrac34}$.

We can now rewrite the integrand and integrate the expression:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int\dfrac{2}{x(3x-8)}\,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int\left(\dfrac{\blueD A}{x}+\dfrac{\maroonD B}{3x-8}\right)\, dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int\left(\dfrac{\blueD{-\dfrac14}}{x}+\dfrac{\maroonD{\dfrac34}}{3x-8}\, \right)dx
\\\\
&=-\dfrac14\displaystyle\int\dfrac{1}{x}\, dx+\dfrac34\cdot\goldD{\dfrac13}\int\dfrac{\goldD{3}}{3x-8}\, dx
\\\\
&=-\dfrac14\text{ln}|x|+\dfrac14\text{ln}|3x-8|+C
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle \int\dfrac{2}{x(3x-8)} \,dx =-\dfrac14\text{ln}|x|+\dfrac14\text{ln}|3x-8|+C$

So $P=-\dfrac14$ and $Q=\dfrac14$.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find the values of $P$ and $Q$ that make the following equality true.**

$\displaystyle \int\dfrac{3x+27}{(2x-2)(x+5)} \,dx = P\cdot\ln|2x-2|+Q\cdot\ln|x+5|+C$

$P=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]

$Q=$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]]
hints:
The [[☃ definition 1]] is a rational function where the degree of the numerator is smaller than the degree of the denominator. Also, the denominator is factorable. This suggests that we should rewrite the integrand using partial fractions:

$\displaystyle\int\dfrac{3x+27}{(2x-2)(x+5)}\,dx=\int\left(\dfrac{A}{2x-2}+\dfrac{B}{x+5}\right)\,dx$

Then, we will be able to integrate using the following formula, which is based on the derivative of $\text{ln}(x)$:

$\displaystyle\int\dfrac{1}{mx+n}\,dx=\dfrac1m\cdot\text{ln}|mx+n|+C$

To find $A$ and $B$, we take the following equation and multiply both sides by the common denominator:

$\begin{align}
\displaystyle\dfrac{3x+27}{(2x-2)(x+5)}&=\dfrac{A}{2x-2}+\dfrac{B}{x+5}
\\\\
3x+27&=A(x+5)+B(2x-2)
\end{align}$

Substituting $x=1$, we get $\blueD{A=5}$.

Substituting $x=-5$, we get $\maroonD{B=-1}$.

We can now rewrite the integrand and integrate the expression:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int\dfrac{3x+27}{(2x-2)(x+5)}\,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int\left(\dfrac{\blueD A}{2x-2}+\dfrac{\maroonD B}{x+5}\right)\, dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int\left(\dfrac{\blueD{5}}{2x-2}+\dfrac{\maroonD{-1}}{x+5}\, \right)dx
\\\\
&=5\cdot\goldD{\dfrac12}\displaystyle\int\dfrac{\goldD{2}}{2x-2}\, dx-\int\dfrac{1}{x+5}\, dx
\\\\
&=\dfrac52\text{ln}|2x-2|-\text{ln}|x+5|+C
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle \int\dfrac{3x+27}{(2x-2)(x+5)} \,dx =\dfrac52\text{ln}|2x-2|-\text{ln}|x+5|+C$

So $P=\dfrac52$ and $Q=-1$.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find the values of $P$ and $Q$ that make the following equality true.**

$\displaystyle \int\dfrac{18-12x}{(4x-1)(x-4)} \,dx = P\cdot\ln|4x-1|+Q\cdot\ln|x-4|+C$

$P=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]

$Q=$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]]
hints:
The [[☃ definition 1]] is a rational function where the degree of the numerator is smaller than the degree of the denominator. Also, the denominator is factorable. This suggests that we should rewrite the integrand using partial fractions:

$\displaystyle\int\dfrac{18-12x}{(4x-1)(x-4)}\,dx=\int\left(\dfrac{A}{4x-1}+\dfrac{B}{x-4}\right)\,dx$

Then, we will be able to integrate using the following formula, which is based on the derivative of $\text{ln}(x)$:

$\displaystyle\int\dfrac{1}{mx+n}\,dx=\dfrac1m\cdot\text{ln}|mx+n|+C$

To find $A$ and $B$, we take the following equation and multiply both sides by the common denominator:

$\begin{align}
\displaystyle\dfrac{18-12x}{(4x-1)(x-4)}&=\dfrac{A}{4x-1}+\dfrac{B}{x-4}
\\\\
18-12x&=A(x-4)+B(4x-1)
\end{align}$

Substituting $x=\dfrac14$, we get $\blueD{A=-4}$.

Substituting $x=4$, we get $\maroonD{B=-2}$.

We can now rewrite the integrand and integrate the expression:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int\dfrac{18-12x}{(4x-1)(x-4)}\,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int\left(\dfrac{\blueD A}{4x-1}+\dfrac{\maroonD B}{x-4}\right)\, dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int\left(\dfrac{\blueD{-4}}{4x-1}+\dfrac{\maroonD{-2}}{x-4}\, \right)dx
\\\\
&=-4\cdot\goldD{\dfrac14}\displaystyle\int\dfrac{\goldD{4}}{4x-1}\, dx-2\int\dfrac{1}{x-4}\, dx
\\\\
&=-\text{ln}|4x-1|-2\text{ln}|x-4|+C
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle \int\dfrac{18-12x}{(4x-1)(x-4)} \,dx =-\text{ln}|4x-1|-2\text{ln}|x-4|+C$

So $P=-1$ and $Q=-2$.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find the values of $P$ and $Q$ that make the following equality true.**

$\displaystyle \int\dfrac{x-1}{(2x+5)(x-8)} \,dx = P\cdot\ln|2x+5|+Q\cdot\ln|x-8|+C$

$P=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]

$Q=$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]]
hints:
The [[☃ definition 1]] is a rational function where the degree of the numerator is smaller than the degree of the denominator. Also, the denominator is factorable. This suggests that we should rewrite the integrand using partial fractions:

$\displaystyle\int\dfrac{x-1}{(2x+5)(x-8)}\,dx=\int\left(\dfrac{A}{2x+5}+\dfrac{B}{x-8}\right)\,dx$

Then, we will be able to integrate using the following formula, which is based on the derivative of $\text{ln}(x)$:

$\displaystyle\int\dfrac{1}{mx+n}\,dx=\dfrac1m\cdot\text{ln}|mx+n|+C$

To find $A$ and $B$, we take the following equation and multiply both sides by the common denominator:

$\begin{align}
\displaystyle\dfrac{x-1}{(2x+5)(x-8)}&=\dfrac{A}{2x+5}+\dfrac{B}{x-8}
\\\\
x-1&=A(x-8)+B(2x+5)
\end{align}$

Substituting $x=-\dfrac52$, we get $\blueD{A=\dfrac13}$.

Substituting $x=8$, we get $\maroonD{B=\dfrac13}$.

We can now rewrite the integrand and integrate the expression:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\int\dfrac{x-1}{(2x+5)(x-8)}\,dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int\left(\dfrac{\blueD A}{2x+5}+\dfrac{\maroonD B}{x-8}\right)\, dx
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\int\left(\dfrac{\blueD{\dfrac13}}{2x+5}+\dfrac{\maroonD{\dfrac13}}{x-8}\, \right)dx
\\\\
&=\dfrac13\cdot\goldD{\dfrac12}\displaystyle\int\dfrac{\goldD{2}}{2x+5}\, dx+\dfrac13\int\dfrac{1}{x-8}\, dx
\\\\
&=\dfrac16\text{ln}|2x+5|+\dfrac13\text{ln}|x-8|+C
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle \int\dfrac{x-1}{(2x+5)(x-8)} \,dx =\dfrac16\text{ln}|2x+5|+\dfrac13\text{ln}|x-8|+C$

So $P=\dfrac16$ and $Q=\dfrac13$.
